content:
# Galatians Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/GAL_002_page_001.jpg

1. Then after fourteen years I went up again to Jerusalem with Barnabas, taking Titus along with me.

2. I went up because of a revelation, and I set before them the gospel that I proclaim among the Gentiles — though privately before those who seemed influential — so that I would not be running or had not run in vain.

3. But even Titus, who was with me, was not compelled to be circumcised, though he was a Greek.

4. Yet because of false brothers secretly brought in — who slipped in to spy on our freedom that we have in Christ Jesus, so that they might bring us into slavery —

5. to them we did not yield in submission even for a moment, so that the truth of the gospel might be preserved for you.

6. And from those who seemed to be influential — what they were makes no difference to me; God shows no partiality — those, I say, who seemed influential added nothing to me.

7. On the contrary, when they saw that I had been entrusted with the gospel to the uncircumcised, just as Peter had been entrusted with the gospel to the circumcised —

8. for he who worked through Peter for his apostolic ministry to the circumcised worked also through me for mine to the Gentiles —

9. and when James and Cephas and John, who seemed to be pillars, perceived the grace that was given to me, they gave the right hand of fellowship to Barnabas and me, agreeing that we should go to the Gentiles and they to the circumcised.

10. Only, they asked us to remember the poor, the very thing I was eager to do.

11. But when Cephas came to Antioch, I opposed him to his face, because he stood condemned.

12. For before certain men came from James, he was eating with the Gentiles; but when they came he drew back and separated himself, fearing the circumcision party.

13. And the rest of the Jews acted hypocritically along with him, so that even Barnabas was led astray by their hypocrisy.

14. But when I saw that their conduct was not in step with the truth of the gospel, I said to Cephas before them all, "If you, though a Jew, live like a Gentile and not like a Jew, how can you force the Gentiles to live like Jews?"

15. We ourselves are Jews by birth and not Gentile sinners;

16. yet we know that a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ, so we also have believed in Christ Jesus, in order to be justified by faith in Christ and not by works of the law, because by works of the law no one will be justified.

17. But if, in our endeavor to be justified in Christ, we too were found to be sinners, is Christ then a servant of sin? Certainly not!

18. For if I rebuild what I tore down, I prove myself to be a transgressor.

19. For through the law I died to the law, so that I might live to God.

20. I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. And the life I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.

21. I do not nullify the grace of God, for if righteousness were through the law, then Christ died for no purpose.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 2 contains three major sections: the Jerusalem Council (1-10), the Antioch Incident (11-14), and the theological argument on justification (15-21). All three translators correctly identified these structural divisions.

**Verse 20 — "I have been crucified with Christ":** All three translators preserved the paradoxical structure of this verse. SOPHIA affirms: this is the theological center of Galatians and one of the most important christological statements in the Pauline corpus.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.2 — "according to a revelation" vs. "because of a revelation" vs. "God revealed to me that I should go"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I went up according to a revelation" | kata apokalypsin — literal |
| LOGOS | "I went up because of a revelation" | Causal rendering |
| PNEUMA | "I went there because God revealed to me that I should go" | Full expansion; identifies God as revealer |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kata + accusative can indicate "according to" or "because of." LOGOS captures the causal sense. PNEUMA expands into a full clause and identifies God as the source of revelation — implied but not stated.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands beyond the text.

---

### v.2 — "running in vain" — structural handling

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "lest somehow I should be running or had run in vain" | Preserves subjunctive and tenses |
| LOGOS | "so that I would not be running or had not run in vain" | Same structure, smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "I wanted to make sure that we were in agreement, for fear that all my efforts had been wasted" | Full paraphrase; "efforts wasted" for "run in vain" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The athletic metaphor ("running") is Paul's standard image for ministry effort. PNEUMA replaces it with "efforts had been wasted" — losing the metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses the athletic metaphor.

---

### v.3 — Sentence structure and emphasis

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But not even Titos, the [one] with me, being a Greek, was compelled to be circumcised" | Literal; participial "being a Greek" |
| LOGOS | "But even Titus, who was with me, was not compelled to be circumcised, though he was a Greek" | Concessive "though" — shifts emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "They did not even demand that my companion Titus, who is a Gentile, be circumcised" | "Demand" for "compel"; "Gentile" for "Greek" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA changes "Greek" (Hellēn) to "Gentile" — a shift from ethnic to religious category. Paul specifically says Hellēn (Greek), which is more precise. PNEUMA also restructures with "demand" and adds "They supported my position" (not in Greek) as a preceding sentence.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA changes "Greek" to "Gentile" and adds content.

---

### v.4 — "false brothers" vs. "so-called believers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the false brothers, secretly brought in" | pseudadelphous — literal compound |
| LOGOS | "false brothers secretly brought in" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "some so-called believers who had been secretly brought in" | "So-called believers" for pseudadelphous |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "False brothers" is the literal and standard rendering. PNEUMA's "so-called believers" captures the sense but loses the familial language — Paul calls them brothers (adelphoi) precisely to highlight the betrayal.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses the kinship language.

---

### v.6 — "God does not receive the face of a man" vs. "God shows no partiality" vs. "God has no favorites"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God does not receive [the] face of a man" | Literal Hebraism |
| LOGOS | "God shows no partiality" | Standard idiomatic rendering |
| PNEUMA | "God has no favorites" | Colloquial rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebraism prosopon lambanein ("receive the face") = "show partiality." LOGOS renders the meaning accurately. PNEUMA's "God has no favorites" is colloquial but adequate. EMET preserves the Semitic idiom.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — idiomatic register.

---

### v.7-8 — "gospel of the uncircumcision/circumcision" vs. "gospel to the uncircumcised/circumcised" vs. "preaching to the Gentiles/Jews"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the gospel of the uncircumcision...of the circumcision" | Genitive — abstract nouns |
| LOGOS | "the gospel to the uncircumcised...to the circumcised" | Renders as audience |
| PNEUMA | "preaching the gospel to the Gentiles...preaching to the Jews" | "Gentiles/Jews" for uncircumcised/circumcised |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The genitive in "gospel of the uncircumcision" could mean "gospel about uncircumcision" or "gospel directed to the uncircumcised." LOGOS correctly renders as audience. PNEUMA replaces "uncircumcised/circumcised" with "Gentiles/Jews" — a valid interpretation but loses the body-marking language that is central to the Galatian controversy.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA loses the circumcision language that is central to the letter's argument.

---

### v.9 — Name order: "Iakobos and Kephas and Ioannes" vs. "James and Cephas and John" vs. "James, Peter, and John"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Iakobos and Kephas and Ioannes" | Transliterated; polysyndeton preserved |
| LOGOS | "James and Cephas and John" | Standard names; polysyndeton preserved |
| PNEUMA | "James, Peter, and John" | Commas; "Peter" for Kephas |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Sinaiticus reads Kephas (Aramaic), not Petros (Greek). LOGOS preserves "Cephas." PNEUMA substitutes "Peter" — a different name form. The polysyndeton ("and...and...and") is also preserved in LOGOS but replaced with commas in PNEUMA.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Cephas vs. Peter — different name forms.

---

### v.11 — "stood condemned" vs. "what he did was very wrong"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he stood condemned" | kategnōsmenos ēn — perfect passive participle |
| LOGOS | "he stood condemned" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "what he did was very wrong" | Paraphrase; adds "very" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kategnōsmenos ēn is a strong juridical term — "he stood condemned/self-condemned." PNEUMA softens it to a moral judgment ("very wrong") rather than the legal/judicial language Paul uses.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA softens juridical language.

---

### v.14 — "walking straight toward the truth" vs. "not in step with" vs. "not following the truth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they were not walking straight toward the truth of the gospel" | orthopodousin — "walk straight/uprightly" |
| LOGOS | "their conduct was not in step with the truth of the gospel" | "In step with" — metaphorical |
| PNEUMA | "they were not following the truth of the gospel message" | "Following"; adds "message" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. orthopodeo (only here in NT) literally means "walk with straight feet" — a vivid metaphor. "In step with" captures the walking imagery. PNEUMA's "following" loses the bodily metaphor and adds "message."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. orthopodeo — walking metaphor preserved.

---

### v.16 — "faith of Christos" vs. "faith in Jesus Christ" vs. "faith in Jesus Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "faith of Yeshua Christos...faith of Christos" | Preserves genitive ambiguity |
| LOGOS | "faith in Jesus Christ...faith in Christ" | Objective genitive — human faith directed at Christ |
| PNEUMA | "faith in Jesus Christ...faith in Christ" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. pistis Christou is one of the most debated constructions in Pauline theology. Objective genitive ("faith in Christ") = human trust directed toward Christ. Subjective genitive ("faithfulness of Christ") = Christ's own faithfulness. EMET preserves the ambiguity; LOGOS and PNEUMA both choose the objective genitive. The consensus follows the majority.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. pistis Christou — "faith in Christ" (objective genitive) vs. "faithfulness of Christ" (subjective genitive). This affects whether justification depends on human faith or Christ's faithfulness. Both readings have strong scholarly support.

---

### v.16 — "no flesh will be justified" vs. "no one will be justified" vs. "no one will ever be made right"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from works of law no flesh will be justified" | sarx — "flesh"; Psalm 143:2 echo |
| LOGOS | "by works of the law no one will be justified" | "No one" for "no flesh" |
| PNEUMA | "no one will ever be made right with God by obeying the law" | "Ever"; "made right with God"; "obeying the law" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "No flesh" (pasa sarx) is a Hebraism meaning "no human being" (cf. Psalm 143:2 LXX). LOGOS renders the meaning. PNEUMA adds "ever" (not in Greek), "with God" (implied but not stated), and changes "works of law" to "obeying the law."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds intensifiers and interpretive content.

---

### v.17 — "May it never be!" vs. "Certainly not!" vs. "Absolutely not!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "May it never be!" | mē genoito — optative of wish |
| LOGOS | "Certainly not!" | Emphatic denial |
| PNEUMA | "Absolutely not!" | Same emphatic sense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. mē genoito is Paul's characteristic exclamation (14x in his letters). All three renderings capture the force. "Certainly not!" is the standard formal rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor — exclamation rendering.

---

### v.19 — "I through law died to law" vs. "through the law I died to the law" vs. expanded explanation

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For I through law died to law" | Anarthrous nomos — no article |
| LOGOS | "For through the law I died to the law" | Adds articles for English clarity |
| PNEUMA | "For when I tried to keep the law, it condemned me. So I died to the law — I stopped trying to meet all its requirements — so that I might live for God" | Full expansion; "condemned me" added; "stopped trying" as gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA adds an entire interpretive expansion — "when I tried to keep the law, it condemned me" and "I stopped trying to meet all its requirements" are not in the Greek. Paul's compressed statement "through the law I died to the law" is deliberately paradoxical and should remain so.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. PNEUMA's expansion resolves a paradox Paul left intentionally compressed. "Through the law I died to the law" is one of Paul's most enigmatic statements — does the law itself bring about death to the law? PNEUMA's explanation is one interpretation, but it forecloses other possibilities.

---

### v.20 — "faith of the Son of God" vs. "faith in the Son of God" vs. "trusting in the Son of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I live by faith — the [faith] of the Son of God" | Preserves genitive |
| LOGOS | "I live by faith in the Son of God" | Objective genitive |
| PNEUMA | "I live in this earthly body by trusting in the Son of God" | "Trusting in"; adds "earthly" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Same pistis + genitive construction as v.16. EMET preserves the ambiguity. LOGOS and PNEUMA both render as objective genitive. PNEUMA adds "earthly" to "body" (not in Greek) and uses "trusting" rather than "faith."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Same pistis Christou issue as v.16.

---

### v.21 — "set aside" vs. "nullify" vs. "treats as meaningless"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I do not set aside the grace of God" | atheteo — "set aside/reject" |
| LOGOS | "I do not nullify the grace of God" | "Nullify" — legal term |
| PNEUMA | "I am not one of those who treats the grace of God as meaningless" | Expanded; "one of those who" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. atheteo means "to set aside, annul, nullify" — a legal term for voiding a contract. "Nullify" captures the legal register. PNEUMA's expansion ("one of those who treats...as meaningless") adds a comparative element not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds comparative framing.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 6 | "God revealed to me," "efforts wasted," "so-called believers," "very wrong," "condemned me," "earthly body" |
| PNEUMA structural changes | 3 | Paraphrases paradox (v.19), replaces circumcision language (v.7-8), adds comparative framing (v.21) |
| pistis Christou genitive | 2 | "Faith of Christ" vs. "faith in Christ" (vv.16, 20) |
| EMET transliteration choices | 2 | Iakobos/Kephas/Ioannes, Christos — preserves foreign forms |
| Idiomatic rendering | 2 | "Receive the face" (v.6), "no flesh" (v.16) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 2 contains two of the most theologically significant disagreements in Galatians: the pistis Christou genitive (vv.16, 20) and PNEUMA's expansion of the compressed paradox "through the law I died to the law" (v.19). The pistis Christou debate — whether justification rests on human faith in Christ or on Christ's own faithfulness — is one of the most consequential grammatical questions in the New Testament. PNEUMA continues to add interpretive content and resolve ambiguities that Paul intentionally left open.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
